skills.teks_description: Compare and contrast prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells, including their complexity
skills.prompt: This topic covers cell structure and function for Biology students (TEKS B.5(B)). Students should understand the differences between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells, including complexity, organelles, and organization. Key concepts: cell membrane structure (phospholipid bilayer, fluid mosaic model), nucleus and genetic material, mitochondria and energy production, ribosomes and protein synthesis, endoplasmic reticulum (rough and smooth), Golgi apparatus, lysosomes, and cytoskeleton. Compare prokaryotic cells (no membrane-bound organelles, circular DNA, smaller) with eukaryotic cells (membrane-bound organelles, linear chromosomes, larger). Plant cells have additional structures: cell wall, chloroplasts, and large central vacuole. Vocabulary: organelle, cytoplasm, nucleoid, plasmid, endosymbiotic theory, compartmentalization. Students should connect cell structure to function and understand how compartmentalization allows for specialized chemical environments. Common misconceptions: all cells look the same; prokaryotes are primitive and simple (they are highly adapted); plant cells do not have mitochondria (they do).

skills.teks_description: Investigate homeostasis through molecular cellular transport
skills.prompt: This topic covers cell transport mechanisms for Biology students (TEKS B.5(C)). Students should understand how cells maintain homeostasis through molecular transport across membranes. Key concepts: passive transport (diffusion, osmosis, facilitated diffusion) requires no energy and moves substances down concentration gradients; active transport (sodium-potassium pump, endocytosis, exocytosis) requires ATP and moves substances against concentration gradients. Osmotic conditions: hypertonic (cell shrinks), hypotonic (cell swells), isotonic (equilibrium). Understand the selectively permeable nature of the cell membrane and how size, polarity, and charge affect transport. Vocabulary: concentration gradient, equilibrium, osmosis, plasmolysis, lysis, turgor pressure, vesicle, pinocytosis, phagocytosis, channel protein, carrier protein. Students should be able to predict cell behavior in different solutions and explain how transport maintains cellular homeostasis. Common misconceptions: osmosis only involves water moving into cells; diffusion stops when molecules are evenly distributed (it reaches dynamic equilibrium).

skills.teks_description: Explain matter conservation and energy transfer during photosynthesis and cellular respiration
skills.prompt: This topic covers photosynthesis and cellular respiration for Biology students (TEKS B.11(A)). Students should understand how energy flows through living systems via these complementary processes. Photosynthesis: 6CO2 + 6H2O + light energy → C6H12O6 + 6O2. Occurs in chloroplasts; light-dependent reactions (thylakoid membranes) produce ATP and NADPH; Calvin cycle (stroma) fixes carbon into glucose. Cellular respiration: C6H12O6 + 6O2 → 6CO2 + 6H2O + ATP. Occurs in mitochondria; glycolysis (cytoplasm), Krebs cycle (matrix), electron transport chain (inner membrane). Key concepts: conservation of matter, energy transformation (light to chemical to usable ATP), role of enzymes, ATP as energy currency. Vocabulary: chlorophyll, thylakoid, stroma, glycolysis, pyruvate, acetyl-CoA, NADH, FADH2, oxidative phosphorylation, chemiosmosis, anaerobic respiration, fermentation. Common misconceptions: plants only photosynthesize (they also respire); photosynthesis and respiration are exact opposites (they differ in pathways and efficiency).

skills.teks_description: Explain the cell cycle significance, including DNA replication and mitosis stages
skills.prompt: This topic covers cellular reproduction for Biology students (TEKS B.6(A)). Students should understand the cell cycle and both mitosis and meiosis. Cell cycle phases: G1 (growth), S (DNA synthesis), G2 (preparation), M (mitosis). Mitosis produces two genetically identical diploid daughter cells for growth and repair; phases: prophase, metaphase, anaphase, telophase, cytokinesis. Meiosis produces four genetically unique haploid gametes; includes two divisions (meiosis I separates homologous chromosomes, meiosis II separates sister chromatids). Key concepts: crossing over and independent assortment increase genetic diversity, checkpoints regulate the cell cycle, cancer results from uncontrolled cell division. Vocabulary: chromatin, chromosome, centromere, spindle fiber, homologous chromosomes, tetrad, synapsis, chiasma, diploid (2n), haploid (n), gamete, somatic cell. Common misconceptions: mitosis and meiosis produce the same type of cells; crossing over happens in mitosis.

skills.teks_description: Identify DNA components and how nucleotide sequences specify traits
skills.prompt: This topic covers DNA structure and replication for Biology students (TEKS B.7(A)). Students should understand the double helix structure of DNA, including the sugar-phosphate backbone and complementary base pairing (adenine-thymine, guanine-cytosine). Key concepts: DNA as the molecule of heredity, the semi-conservative model of replication, roles of enzymes (helicase unwinds, primase adds RNA primer, DNA polymerase adds nucleotides, ligase seals gaps), leading and lagging strands, and Okazaki fragments. Students should understand why accurate replication is essential for cell division and how errors (mutations) can occur. Nucleotide structure: deoxyribose sugar, phosphate group, nitrogenous base (purines: A, G; pyrimidines: C, T). Vocabulary: nucleotide, base pair, complementary, antiparallel, replication fork, helicase, polymerase, ligase, semi-conservative, template strand, 5' to 3' direction. Common misconceptions: DNA replication produces two completely new molecules (it is semi-conservative); all mutations are harmful (many are neutral).

skills.teks_description: Describe gene expression and protein synthesis using models
skills.prompt: This topic covers RNA and protein synthesis for Biology students (TEKS B.7(B)). Students should understand the central dogma: DNA → RNA → Protein. Transcription: DNA is used as a template to produce mRNA in the nucleus; RNA polymerase reads the template strand 3' to 5' and builds mRNA 5' to 3'. RNA uses uracil instead of thymine. mRNA processing: 5' cap, poly-A tail, splicing of introns. Translation: ribosomes read mRNA codons (three-nucleotide sequences) and tRNA brings corresponding amino acids; occurs at ribosomes in the cytoplasm. Key concepts: codon-anticodon pairing, start codon (AUG/methionine), stop codons (UAA, UAG, UGA), genetic code is universal and degenerate. Types of RNA: mRNA (messenger), tRNA (transfer), rRNA (ribosomal). Vocabulary: transcription, translation, codon, anticodon, amino acid, polypeptide, exon, intron, promoter, terminator. Common misconceptions: DNA leaves the nucleus; each codon codes for a unique amino acid (the code is degenerate).

skills.teks_description: Predict genetic outcomes using monohybrid and dihybrid crosses
skills.prompt: This topic covers Mendelian genetics for Biology students (TEKS B.8(B)). Students should understand Gregor Mendel's laws of inheritance and how to predict genetic outcomes. Law of Segregation: each organism has two alleles for each trait that separate during gamete formation. Law of Independent Assortment: genes on different chromosomes sort independently. Key concepts: dominant vs. recessive alleles, genotype vs. phenotype, homozygous vs. heterozygous, Punnett squares for monohybrid (one trait) and dihybrid (two traits) crosses, probability ratios (3:1 for monohybrid F2, 9:3:3:1 for dihybrid F2). Test crosses identify unknown genotypes. Vocabulary: allele, locus, gene, trait, P generation, F1 generation, F2 generation, gamete, hybrid, true-breeding, carrier. Students should be able to set up and solve Punnett squares, calculate phenotypic and genotypic ratios, and apply probability to genetics problems. Common misconceptions: dominant traits are more common in populations; blending inheritance is correct.

skills.teks_description: Predict genetic outcomes including non-Mendelian traits such as incomplete dominance and codominance
skills.prompt: This topic covers non-Mendelian genetics for Biology students (TEKS B.8(B)). Students should understand inheritance patterns that do not follow simple Mendelian rules. Key patterns: incomplete dominance (heterozygote shows blended phenotype, e.g., red x white = pink), codominance (both alleles fully expressed, e.g., AB blood type), multiple alleles (more than two alleles exist in the population, e.g., ABO blood types with IA, IB, i), sex-linked inheritance (genes on X chromosome, males more affected), polygenic inheritance (multiple genes influence one trait, e.g., skin color, height), epistasis (one gene masks expression of another). Key concepts: X-linked recessive conditions (color blindness, hemophilia) appear more frequently in males; pedigree analysis can trace inheritance patterns through families. Vocabulary: incomplete dominance, codominance, multiple alleles, sex-linked, autosomal, carrier, polygenic, epistasis, pedigree, X-linked. Common misconceptions: all traits follow simple dominant-recessive patterns; sex-linked disorders affect only males.

skills.teks_description: Analyze how natural selection produces population change over time
skills.prompt: This topic covers evolution by natural selection for Biology students (TEKS B.10(A)). Students should understand Darwin's theory and how populations change over time. Natural selection mechanism: variation exists in populations, some variations are heritable, organisms produce more offspring than can survive, individuals with advantageous traits are more likely to survive and reproduce (fitness). Key concepts: adaptation, speciation (allopatric and sympatric), evidence for evolution (fossil record, comparative anatomy, molecular biology, biogeography, embryology), types of selection (directional, stabilizing, disruptive). Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium describes conditions when evolution is NOT occurring (no mutation, random mating, no selection, large population, no migration). Vocabulary: natural selection, fitness, adaptation, speciation, homologous structures, analogous structures, vestigial structures, gene pool, allele frequency, genetic drift, gene flow, founder effect, bottleneck effect. Common misconceptions: evolution is goal-directed; individuals evolve (populations evolve); survival of the fittest means strongest.

skills.teks_description: Analyze evidence of common ancestry through fossil records, biogeography, and homologies
skills.prompt: This topic covers classification and taxonomy for Biology students (TEKS B.9(A)). Students should understand how organisms are classified based on evolutionary relationships. Linnaean hierarchy: Domain, Kingdom, Phylum, Class, Order, Family, Genus, Species. Three domains: Bacteria, Archaea, Eukarya. Six kingdoms: Bacteria, Archaea, Protista, Fungi, Plantae, Animalia. Key concepts: binomial nomenclature (Genus species), phylogenetic trees (cladograms) show evolutionary relationships, evidence for classification includes morphology, molecular data (DNA/RNA/protein sequences), fossils, biogeography, and embryological development. Cladistics groups organisms by shared derived characteristics (synapomorphies). Vocabulary: taxonomy, phylogeny, clade, common ancestor, monophyletic, outgroup, derived character, convergent evolution, divergent evolution, analogous structures, homologous structures. Students should be able to read and construct cladograms, identify common ancestors, and use evidence to justify classification decisions. Common misconceptions: classification is fixed and unchanging; more similar-looking organisms are always more closely related.

skills.teks_description: Investigate ecological relationships including predation, parasitism, commensalism, mutualism, and competition
skills.prompt: This topic covers ecology and ecosystems for Biology students (TEKS B.13(A)). Students should understand ecological relationships and how organisms interact within ecosystems. Levels of organization: organism, population, community, ecosystem, biome, biosphere. Ecological relationships: predation (predator-prey dynamics), parasitism (one benefits, one harmed), commensalism (one benefits, other unaffected), mutualism (both benefit), competition (intraspecific and interspecific). Key concepts: food chains and food webs, trophic levels (producers, primary consumers, secondary consumers, tertiary consumers, decomposers), energy transfer (10% rule), biomass pyramids. Vocabulary: niche, habitat, symbiosis, keystone species, trophic cascade, primary productivity, biomass, carrying capacity, limiting factor, competitive exclusion principle. Students should be able to trace energy flow through ecosystems, analyze food webs, and predict effects of removing species. Common misconceptions: ecosystems are static; decomposers are not important to energy flow.

skills.teks_description: Explain how environmental changes affect biodiversity and ecosystem stability
skills.prompt: This topic covers biomes and biodiversity for Biology students (TEKS B.13(D)). Students should understand how environmental changes affect biodiversity and ecosystem stability. Major terrestrial biomes: tropical rainforest (high biodiversity, warm and wet), desert (low precipitation, extreme temperatures), grassland/savanna (dominated by grasses, seasonal rainfall), temperate deciduous forest (four seasons, broad-leaf trees), taiga/boreal forest (coniferous, cold winters), tundra (permafrost, low biodiversity), chaparral (Mediterranean climate). Aquatic biomes: freshwater (lakes, rivers, wetlands) and marine (open ocean, coral reefs, estuaries, intertidal zones). Key concepts: climate determines biome distribution (temperature and precipitation), biodiversity varies by biome, human activities threaten biodiversity (habitat destruction, pollution, invasive species, climate change). Vocabulary: biome, biodiversity, climate, latitude, altitude, permafrost, canopy, understory, photic zone, aphotic zone, estuary, littoral zone. Common misconceptions: deserts are always hot; rainforests have the most fertile soil.

skills.teks_description: Analyze disruptions to matter cycling and energy flow through trophic levels
skills.prompt: This topic covers population dynamics for Biology students (TEKS B.13(B)). Students should understand how populations grow, are regulated, and how energy flows through trophic levels. Population growth models: exponential growth (J-curve, unlimited resources) vs. logistic growth (S-curve, limited resources approaching carrying capacity K). Growth rate equation: dN/dt = rN for exponential; dN/dt = rN((K-N)/K) for logistic. Key concepts: density-dependent limiting factors (competition, predation, disease, parasitism) vs. density-independent factors (natural disasters, climate, weather), carrying capacity, survivorship curves (Type I, II, III), age structure diagrams predict future population growth. Trophic levels and energy flow: only about 10% of energy transfers between trophic levels. Vocabulary: population density, dispersion, immigration, emigration, birth rate, death rate, carrying capacity, r-strategist, K-strategist, demographic transition, survivorship curve, trophic level. Common misconceptions: populations always grow exponentially; carrying capacity is fixed and unchanging.

skills.teks_description: Explain carbon and nitrogen cycle significance and disruption consequences
skills.prompt: This topic covers human impact on the environment for Biology students (TEKS B.13(C)). Students should understand biogeochemical cycles and how human activities disrupt them. Carbon cycle: carbon moves through atmosphere (CO2), organisms (photosynthesis/respiration), oceans, and fossil fuels; burning fossil fuels increases atmospheric CO2 causing enhanced greenhouse effect. Nitrogen cycle: nitrogen fixation (N2 to NH3 by bacteria), nitrification, denitrification; human impacts include fertilizer runoff causing eutrophication and algal blooms. Water cycle: evaporation, condensation, precipitation, transpiration, runoff; pollution affects water quality. Phosphorus cycle: rocks to soil to organisms; excess phosphorus from agriculture causes water pollution. Key concepts: greenhouse gases and global warming, deforestation effects, ozone depletion, acid rain, biomagnification, invasive species, habitat fragmentation, conservation strategies. Vocabulary: biogeochemical cycle, carbon sink, nitrogen fixation, eutrophication, biomagnification, greenhouse effect, deforestation, sustainability. Common misconceptions: the greenhouse effect is entirely bad (it is natural and necessary); ozone depletion causes global warming.

skills.skill: Anatomy and Physiology of Major Systems
skills.teks_description: Analyze animal system interactions for regulation, nutrient absorption, reproduction, and defense
skills.prompt: This topic covers anatomy and physiology of major body systems for Biology students (TEKS B.12(A)). Students should understand how animal body systems interact to maintain homeostasis. Key systems: circulatory (heart, blood vessels, blood; transports O2, nutrients, waste), respiratory (lungs, gas exchange; O2 in, CO2 out), digestive (mechanical and chemical breakdown; mouth, stomach, intestines), nervous (brain, spinal cord, neurons; electrochemical signals), endocrine (hormones, glands; chemical regulation), excretory/urinary (kidneys, nephrons; waste removal, water balance), musculoskeletal (bones, muscles; support and movement), reproductive (gamete production, fertilization), immune (defense against pathogens). Key concepts: feedback loops (negative feedback maintains homeostasis, positive feedback amplifies response), systems work together for regulation, nutrient absorption, reproduction, and defense. Vocabulary: homeostasis, organ system, tissue, feedback loop, hormone, neuron, synapse, antibody, antigen. Common misconceptions: each body system works independently; the heart pumps only oxygenated blood.

skills.teks_description: Compare viral versus cellular structures and explain disease transmission
skills.prompt: This topic covers viruses and bacteria for Biology students (TEKS B.5(D)). Students should understand the structural differences between viruses and cells, and how diseases are transmitted. Viruses: not considered living, composed of nucleic acid (DNA or RNA) surrounded by a protein coat (capsid), some have lipid envelopes; reproduce only inside host cells (obligate intracellular parasites). Viral reproduction: lytic cycle (immediate replication and cell lysis) and lysogenic cycle (viral DNA integrates into host genome). Bacteria: prokaryotic cells, circular DNA, no membrane-bound organelles, cell wall (peptidoglycan in most), reproduce by binary fission. Shapes: coccus (sphere), bacillus (rod), spirillum (spiral). Key concepts: antibiotics target bacteria not viruses, antibiotic resistance through natural selection, beneficial bacteria (gut microbiome, decomposition, nitrogen fixation), disease transmission (direct contact, airborne, vector-borne, waterborne). Vocabulary: pathogen, capsid, bacteriophage, lytic, lysogenic, binary fission, antibiotic resistance, vaccine, epidemic, pandemic. Common misconceptions: antibiotics work on viruses; all bacteria are harmful.

skills.teks_description: Analyze animal system interactions including defense and immune response mechanisms
skills.prompt: This topic covers the immune system for Biology students (TEKS B.12(A)). Students should understand how the body defends against pathogens through multiple defense mechanisms. First line of defense: physical and chemical barriers (skin, mucous membranes, stomach acid, tears, saliva). Second line of defense: nonspecific innate immune response (inflammation, fever, phagocytes like macrophages and neutrophils, natural killer cells, complement proteins). Third line of defense: adaptive (specific) immune response involving lymphocytes. B cells produce antibodies (humoral immunity); T cells attack infected cells directly (cell-mediated immunity). Key concepts: antigen recognition, antibody specificity (lock-and-key), memory cells provide long-term immunity, active immunity (vaccination or infection) vs. passive immunity (antibodies transferred), autoimmune diseases (immune system attacks self), allergies (overreaction to harmless substances), immunodeficiency. Vocabulary: antigen, antibody, lymphocyte, B cell, T cell (helper, cytotoxic, memory), phagocyte, inflammation, vaccine, pathogen, immune response. Common misconceptions: vaccines cause the disease; the immune system is one single organ.

skills.teks_description: Explain plant system interactions involving transport, reproduction, and response to environment
skills.prompt: This topic covers plant structure and function for Biology students (TEKS B.12(B)). Students should understand how plant systems interact for transport, reproduction, and environmental response. Plant tissue types: dermal (protection), vascular (transport), ground (photosynthesis, storage, support). Vascular tissue: xylem (transports water and minerals upward, driven by transpiration pull and cohesion-tension) and phloem (transports sugars bidirectionally via translocation and pressure flow). Plant organs: roots (absorption, anchorage), stems (support, transport), leaves (photosynthesis, gas exchange through stomata). Reproduction: alternation of generations (sporophyte and gametophyte), flower structure (sepals, petals, stamens, pistil), pollination, fertilization, seed and fruit development. Plant responses: tropisms (phototropism, gravitropism, thigmotropism), hormones (auxin, gibberellin, cytokinin, ethylene, abscisic acid), photoperiodism. Vocabulary: transpiration, translocation, meristem, cambium, stomata, guard cell, xylem, phloem, germination, tropism, hormone. Common misconceptions: plants get food from soil; water moves up through plants by capillary action alone.

skills.teks_description: Discuss PCR, gel electrophoresis, and genetic engineering applications
skills.prompt: This topic covers biotechnology for Biology students (TEKS B.7(D)). Students should understand modern biotechnology tools and their applications. PCR (Polymerase Chain Reaction): amplifies specific DNA segments using primers, DNA polymerase, and thermal cycling (denature at 95C, anneal primers at 55C, extend at 72C); produces millions of copies. Gel electrophoresis: separates DNA fragments by size using an electric field through agarose gel; smaller fragments move farther; used for DNA fingerprinting, forensics, and paternity testing. Genetic engineering: using restriction enzymes to cut DNA at specific recognition sequences, ligase to join DNA fragments, plasmids as vectors to insert genes into organisms, recombinant DNA technology. Applications: gene therapy, transgenic organisms (GMOs), pharmaceutical production (insulin, growth hormone), forensic identification, agricultural improvements. Vocabulary: PCR, primer, thermal cycler, gel electrophoresis, restriction enzyme, ligase, plasmid, vector, recombinant DNA, transgenic, clone, GMO, CRISPR. Common misconceptions: GMOs are inherently dangerous; cloning produces identical organisms in every way.

skills.teks_description: Discuss PCR, gel electrophoresis, and genetic engineering applications and implications
skills.prompt: This topic covers genetic engineering for Biology students (TEKS B.7(D)). Students should understand advanced genetic engineering techniques, applications, and ethical implications. Key techniques: recombinant DNA technology (combining DNA from different sources), gene cloning (inserting genes into vectors like plasmids for replication in host organisms), CRISPR-Cas9 (precise gene editing using guide RNA and Cas9 enzyme to cut DNA at specific locations). Applications: medical (gene therapy for genetic disorders, production of human insulin in bacteria, development of vaccines), agricultural (herbicide-resistant crops, Bt crops producing insect-resistant proteins, golden rice with enhanced vitamin A), industrial (biofuels, bioremediation, enzyme production). Ethical considerations: safety of GMO consumption, environmental concerns (gene flow to wild populations, biodiversity loss), designer babies debate, intellectual property of genetically modified organisms, equitable access to genetic technologies. Vocabulary: CRISPR, Cas9, guide RNA, gene therapy, transgenic organism, knockout gene, gene drive, bioethics, informed consent, bioremediation. Students should evaluate both benefits and risks of genetic engineering technologies.
